mappings.rationaleAI: Aristotle's systematic study of animals including classification, comparative anatomy, and teleological explanations of biological function directly covers Aristotelian concepts of classification, reproduction, heredity, and descent in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.b).

The 2000-year history of anatomical knowledge from Hippocratic and Galenic traditions through Vesalius's 1543 treatise covers initial anatomical discoveries in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.b), medieval developments (A.1.c), and the Renaissance scientific study of anatomy through dissection (A.1.d).

Galen's anatomical studies, integration of Hippocratic medicine with Aristotelian philosophy, and his authority persisting through the medieval period cover biological ideas in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.b) and the influence of Arabian biologists in the Middle Ages (A.1.c).
The history of ideas about the heart from Aristotle's view as the seat of intelligence through Galen's theories covers biological theories about mankind in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.b).
Pliny's Natural History covers zoology, botany, and natural history in the Greco-Roman world, providing a comprehensive compilation of biological knowledge from antiquity (A.1.b).

The discovery of cells by Hooke and the development of cell theory represent foundational developments in biological science that emerged from the Renaissance tradition of microscopic observation (A.1.d).

Darwin's post-Origin work on orchid pollination, emotional expression, earthworms, and sexual selection illustrates the development of biological ideas through meticulous empirical work across diverse fields (A.1.b broadly as a continuation of systematic biological investigation).

The history of ideas about the eye and vision from ancient theories through Darwin's discussion of eye evolution covers biological theories in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.b) and botanical/zoological investigations.
The fish-tetrapod transition illustrates evolutionary biology and comparative anatomy relevant to the development of biological ideas about the origin and development of life (A.1.b).
The evolutionary history of whales from land-dwelling mammals to aquatic giants represents a major case study in macroevolution and adaptation relevant to theories about descent (A.1.b).
